modules.title: C++ Fundamentals: Syntax, Types & Compilation

sections.heading: What is C++ and Why It Still Matters
sections.content: C++ is a statically-typed, compiled, multi-paradigm language created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1979 as 'C with Classes'. It dominates domains where performance and control matter: game engines (Unreal, Unity's core), browsers (Chrome, Firefox), databases (MySQL, MongoDB), HFT systems, embedded firmware, and operating systems. Unlike Java or Python, C++ gives you direct memory control with zero-overhead abstractions — you only pay for what you use. Modern C++ (C++11 and later) added smart pointers, lambdas, move semantics, and a rich standard library that makes it both safer and more expressive.
sections.tip: Always compile with `-Wall -Wextra` (and `-Werror` in CI). Most C++ bugs are caught by warnings — ignoring them is how juniors ship segfaults to production.

sections.heading: Compilation Pipeline: Preprocessor → Compiler → Linker
sections.content: C++ has a multi-stage build process. The preprocessor handles `#include`, `#define`, and `#ifdef` — it textually expands headers into your source. The compiler turns each `.cpp` (translation unit) into an object file (`.o`). The linker combines object files plus libraries into the final executable, resolving symbol references between them. Understanding this is critical: 'undefined reference' errors are linker failures (missing definition); 'undeclared identifier' errors are compiler failures (missing declaration).
sections.tip: Interview classic: 'What's the difference between declaration and definition?' Declaration tells the compiler something exists; definition allocates storage / provides the body. You can declare many times but define exactly once (One Definition Rule).

sections.heading: Primitive Types and Fixed-Width Integers
sections.content: C++ inherits C's primitive types: `int`, `char`, `short`, `long`, `long long`, `float`, `double`, `bool`. Their exact size depends on the platform — `int` is usually 32 bits but isn't guaranteed. For portability and explicit intent, prefer fixed-width integers from `<cstdint>`: `int32_t`, `uint64_t`, `int8_t`. Use `size_t` (unsigned) for sizes and indices. Use `auto` to let the compiler infer types when it's obvious.
sections.tip: Never assume `int` is 32 bits when interfacing with files, networks, or hardware. Use `int32_t` / `uint32_t` so your code behaves identically on every platform.

sections.heading: Variables, References, and Const-Correctness
sections.content: C++ has three ways to bind a name: by value (copy), by reference (alias to existing variable), and by pointer (address). References are safer than pointers — they can't be null and can't be reseated. `const` is one of C++'s most important features: applied to variables, parameters, member functions, and return types, it locks down what can mutate. 'Const-correctness' (using `const` everywhere it's valid) catches bugs at compile time and documents intent.
sections.tip: Pass by `const T&` for any non-trivial type (string, vector, custom class) to avoid copying. Use `T` (by value) only for primitives and types meant to be moved/copied.

sections.heading: Control Flow and Range-Based For
sections.content: C++ has the standard control flow: `if/else`, `switch`, `while`, `do-while`, and `for`. Modern C++ added the range-based `for` loop, which is shorter and harder to misuse than indexed loops. C++17 added `if`-with-initializer for scoped helper variables. Always prefer range-based `for` when you don't need the index — it works with any container exposing `begin()` and `end()`.
sections.tip: Forgetting `break` in a switch is a classic bug. Use `[[fallthrough]]` to mark intentional fallthrough — the compiler will warn about every other unmarked case.

key_points: C++ is statically typed and compiled to native code via preprocessor → compiler → linker
Always compile with -Wall -Wextra; treat warnings as errors in CI
Use fixed-width types (int32_t, uint64_t) from <cstdint> for portability
Pass by const reference for non-primitives to avoid copies
References can't be null or reseated; pointers can be both
Prefer range-based for loops; use auto when type is obvious
One Definition Rule: declare many times, define exactly once

module_quiz.question: What stage of the build process produces 'undefined reference' errors?
module_quiz.explanation: Linker errors occur when a symbol is declared (compiler is happy) but never defined or the object file containing the definition isn't linked in.

module_quiz.question: Which is the safest way to pass a std::string to a function that only reads it?
module_quiz.explanation: const& avoids the copy AND prevents accidental modification. Plain & allows modification; by value forces a copy.

module_quiz.question: Why prefer int32_t over int for a network protocol field?
options: int's size is implementation-defined; int32_t is exactly 32 bits everywhere
module_quiz.explanation: Different platforms can size `int` differently. For binary formats and protocols, you need exact widths.

module_quiz.question: What does `#pragma once` do?
options: Runs the file once at startup
Equivalent to include guards — prevents the header being included twice in the same translation unit
module_quiz.explanation: It's a non-standard but universally supported alternative to `#ifndef X #define X ... #endif` include guards.

module_quiz.question: Which is true about C++ references?
options: They can be reassigned to refer to a different object
They must be initialized when declared and can never be reseated
module_quiz.explanation: A reference is bound at creation and stays bound. Assigning to a reference modifies the referred-to object, not the reference itself.

modules.title: Functions, Pointers & Memory Management

sections.heading: Function Basics, Overloading, and Default Arguments
sections.content: Functions in C++ have richer features than C. You can overload them — multiple functions with the same name distinguished by parameter types. Default arguments let callers omit trailing parameters. The `inline` keyword hints the compiler to inline the call (mostly advisory now). C++11 added trailing return types (`auto f() -> int`) useful for templates. Always declare functions in headers and define them in `.cpp` files — except templates and inline functions, which must live in headers.
sections.tip: Default arguments are evaluated at the CALL site, not the definition site. Put them in the header declaration; never duplicate in the .cpp definition or you'll get conflicting defaults.

sections.heading: Pointers: Address-of, Dereference, Arithmetic
sections.content: A pointer holds the memory address of another object. `&x` gives the address of x; `*p` dereferences pointer p (accesses the pointed-to value). Pointers can be null (`nullptr` in C++11+, never use NULL). They support arithmetic — `p + 1` advances by `sizeof(*p)` bytes. Array decay: an array name in most contexts converts to a pointer to its first element. Modern C++ prefers smart pointers (`unique_ptr`, `shared_ptr`) for ownership, leaving raw pointers for non-owning observation.
sections.tip: 'Const reads right-to-left' rule: `const int*` = pointer to const int. `int* const` = const pointer to int. `const int* const` = const pointer to const int. Apply this and pointer-const confusion vanishes.

sections.heading: The Heap: new, delete, and Why You Should Avoid Them
sections.content: C++ has two memory regions: the stack (automatic, fast, fixed size) and the heap (dynamic, manual lifetime). Allocate on the heap with `new`, free with `delete`. Forget `delete` → memory leak. Use after `delete` → undefined behavior. Delete twice → double-free crash. These bugs are why modern C++ wraps `new`/`delete` in RAII classes (smart pointers, containers) — you should rarely write raw `new` or `delete` in application code.
sections.tip: If you see raw `new` or `delete` in a code review for a new project, push back. Smart pointers and containers eliminate 90% of memory bugs at zero runtime cost.

sections.heading: RAII: The Most Important C++ Idiom
sections.content: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) is C++'s answer to manual resource management. The idea: tie a resource's lifetime to an object's lifetime. The constructor acquires the resource; the destructor releases it. When the object goes out of scope (even via exception), the destructor runs automatically — guaranteed. This pattern handles memory, files, locks, sockets, DB connections, anything. Smart pointers, std::lock_guard, std::fstream — all RAII.
sections.tip: RAII is THE pattern that makes C++ feel safe. Whenever you see manual cleanup code (free, fclose, unlock, delete), wrap it in a class with a destructor and the bug class disappears.

sections.heading: Smart Pointers: unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr
sections.content: Smart pointers are RAII wrappers around raw pointers. `std::unique_ptr<T>` is a sole owner — non-copyable, movable, and zero overhead vs raw pointers. `std::shared_ptr<T>` uses reference counting — multiple owners, last one frees the object (atomic ops add small overhead). `std::weak_ptr<T>` is a non-owning observer that can check if a shared_ptr is still alive — used to break reference cycles. Always prefer `make_unique` and `make_shared` over direct `new`.
sections.tip: Use unique_ptr by default. Reach for shared_ptr only when you genuinely need shared ownership (rare). shared_ptr's atomic refcount has measurable cost in tight loops.

key_points: Stack = automatic, fast; heap = manual, error-prone
RAII: tie resources to object lifetime; constructors acquire, destructors release
Use std::unique_ptr by default; std::shared_ptr only when shared ownership is needed
Use make_unique / make_shared rather than `new`
Functions can be overloaded by parameter types; default args go in headers
Pointer-const rule: read right-to-left; `const int*` = pointer to const int
Modern C++ rarely uses raw new/delete — wrap in containers and smart pointers

module_quiz.question: Which smart pointer should you reach for by default?
module_quiz.explanation: unique_ptr expresses sole ownership with zero runtime overhead. shared_ptr only when you actually need shared ownership.

module_quiz.question: What is RAII?
options: A pattern where resource lifetimes match object lifetimes — constructor acquires, destructor releases
module_quiz.explanation: RAII is the foundation of safe C++ — it guarantees cleanup even when exceptions occur.

module_quiz.question: What's wrong with `int* arr = new int[10]; delete arr;` ?
options: Should use delete[] for arrays, not delete — undefined behavior otherwise
module_quiz.explanation: Mixing new[] with delete (or new with delete[]) is undefined behavior. Use std::vector<int> instead and avoid the bug entirely.

module_quiz.question: What does std::move do to a unique_ptr?
options: Transfers ownership — source becomes nullptr, destination owns the resource
module_quiz.explanation: unique_ptr is non-copyable; std::move enables explicit ownership transfer.

module_quiz.question: Why use weak_ptr alongside shared_ptr?
options: To break reference cycles that would otherwise leak
module_quiz.explanation: Two shared_ptrs referring to each other never reach refcount 0. weak_ptr observes without owning, breaking the cycle.

modules.title: Object-Oriented Programming in C++

sections.heading: Classes, Members, and Access Specifiers
sections.content: A class bundles data (members) and behavior (member functions). Access specifiers control visibility: `public` (anyone), `private` (only the class itself), `protected` (the class and derived classes). `struct` is identical to `class` except members default to public instead of private. Idiomatic C++: keep data private, expose behavior through public methods, and put member function definitions out-of-line in the .cpp file (except trivial accessors).
sections.tip: Mark every member function that doesn't modify `*this` as `const`. Callers can then invoke them on const objects, and the compiler enforces that they truly don't mutate state.

sections.heading: Constructors, Destructors, and Initializer Lists
sections.content: The constructor runs when an object is created; the destructor when it's destroyed. Always use member initializer lists (`: a(x), b(y)`) instead of assigning in the body — they initialize directly rather than default-construct then assign, and they're required for `const` members and references. C++11 added delegating constructors (one constructor calling another) and `= default` / `= delete` to explicitly request or forbid compiler-generated functions.
sections.tip: Member initializer-list order = declaration order in the class. The order you write in the constructor doesn't matter. Compilers warn with -Wreorder — enable it.

sections.heading: The Rule of Three / Five / Zero
sections.content: If your class manages a resource (raw pointer, file handle, socket), the compiler-generated copy/move operations are usually wrong. Rule of Three (pre-C++11): if you write any of {destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment}, you probably need all three. Rule of Five (C++11+): add move constructor and move assignment. Rule of Zero (best): don't manage resources directly — use vector, string, unique_ptr, etc., and let the compiler-generated defaults work correctly.
sections.tip: Rule of Zero is the goal. If you find yourself writing destructors, copy constructors, etc., ask 'can I use a smart pointer or container instead?' Almost always: yes.

sections.heading: Inheritance and Polymorphism
sections.content: C++ supports single and multiple inheritance. Use `public` inheritance for 'is-a' relationships; the derived class is substitutable for the base. Mark methods you intend to override with `virtual` in the base and `override` in the derived class — `override` makes the compiler verify you're actually overriding (not accidentally creating a new function). Always make destructors `virtual` in classes meant to be inherited from, otherwise `delete base_ptr` won't call the derived destructor → resource leaks.
sections.tip: Always use `override` on derived virtual functions. If the base signature changes, the compiler will catch every now-broken override at compile time instead of silently letting them become new functions.

sections.heading: Operator Overloading
sections.content: C++ lets you define what operators (+, ==, [], <<, etc.) mean for your types. Done well, this makes user-defined types feel like primitives (e.g., `std::string + std::string`). Done poorly, it surprises readers — only overload when the meaning is unambiguous. Common candidates: arithmetic operators for math types, `==` and `<` for comparable types, `<<` for stream printing, `[]` for container-like types. C++20 added `<=>` (spaceship) which generates all comparison operators from one definition.
sections.tip: Overload `<<` as a free function (often a friend) — never as a member, because the left operand is `std::ostream`, not your class. Same for binary operators where the left side might be a non-class type.

key_points: Keep data private; expose behavior via public methods
Mark non-mutating member functions const
Use member initializer lists; init order = declaration order
Rule of Zero: prefer containers/smart pointers so compiler defaults work
Virtual destructor in any class meant to be inherited from
Always use `override` on derived virtual methods
Use `= default` and `= delete` to control compiler-generated functions
C++20 spaceship operator (<=>) auto-generates all comparisons

module_quiz.question: What's the most important reason to declare a base-class destructor virtual?
options: Allows polymorphic deletion via base pointer to call the correct derived destructor
module_quiz.explanation: Without a virtual destructor, `delete basePtr` only runs the base destructor — derived resources leak. It's a subtle, common bug.

module_quiz.question: Member initializer-list order is determined by:
options: The order written in the constructor
module_quiz.explanation: Even if you write `: b(x), a(b)`, `a` is initialized first (declaration order), reading uninitialized `b`. Compile with -Wreorder.

module_quiz.question: What does `= delete` after a constructor do?
options: Tells the compiler not to generate that function and to error on attempts to use it
module_quiz.explanation: `= delete` explicitly forbids a function — useful for non-copyable types: `MyClass(const MyClass&) = delete;`

module_quiz.question: What's the Rule of Zero?
options: A class should have zero members
Don't manage resources directly — use containers and smart pointers so the compiler-generated copy/move/destructor are correct
module_quiz.explanation: By avoiding raw resource ownership, you avoid having to write the Rule of Five boilerplate. Compose from RAII types instead.

module_quiz.question: Why use the `override` keyword on a derived virtual method?
options: Compiler verifies you're actually overriding a virtual base method — catches signature mismatch bugs
module_quiz.explanation: Without `override`, a typo or signature change silently creates a new function rather than an override. `override` makes that a compile error.

modules.title: STL: Containers, Iterators & Algorithms

sections.heading: Sequence Containers: vector, array, deque, list
sections.content: The Standard Template Library (STL) is C++'s answer to data structures. `std::vector<T>` is the workhorse — dynamic array, contiguous storage, amortized O(1) push_back, O(1) random access. Use it 95% of the time. `std::array<T,N>` is a fixed-size compile-time array (replaces C arrays). `std::deque` is a double-ended queue (efficient front and back insertion). `std::list` is a doubly-linked list — rarely the right choice; vectors are faster even for 'lots of insertions' due to cache locality.
sections.tip: When in doubt, use vector. Modern CPUs love contiguous memory. Real benchmarks show vector beating list even for insertion-heavy workloads up to thousands of elements.

sections.heading: Associative Containers: map, set, unordered_map
sections.content: Maps and sets store unique keys. `std::map` and `std::set` are tree-based (red-black tree), keys ordered, O(log n) operations. `std::unordered_map` and `std::unordered_set` are hash-based, O(1) average / O(n) worst case, no ordering. Use unordered for lookups when ordering doesn't matter (faster on average). Multimaps and multisets allow duplicate keys. C++17 added structured bindings and `try_emplace` / `insert_or_assign` for cleaner map manipulation.
sections.tip: `map["key"]` default-constructs the value if the key doesn't exist — surprising behavior. Use `find()` or `contains()` (C++20) when you want to check existence without inserting.

sections.heading: Iterators
sections.content: Iterators generalize pointers — they're the bridge between containers and algorithms. Categories: input (read forward, single pass), output (write forward), forward (multi-pass forward), bidirectional (++ and --), random access (+/-/[]). vector and array iterators are random-access; list iterators are bidirectional; unordered_map iterators are forward-only. `begin()` returns the first element; `end()` returns one-past-the-end (the sentinel — never dereference it). C++20 added 'ranges' which makes much of this cleaner.
sections.tip: Iterator invalidation is a major source of bugs. After `vector.push_back`, all iterators may be invalidated (if reallocation happens). After `map.erase(it)`, only that iterator is invalidated. Know the rules per container.

sections.heading: STL Algorithms
sections.content: The `<algorithm>` header has 100+ generic functions that work on any container via iterators. Common ones: sort, find, count, transform, accumulate, all_of/any_of/none_of, unique, remove_if, copy_if, partition, lower_bound. Most take a predicate (function or lambda). Combined with iterators, this lets you replace dozens of hand-written loops with declarative one-liners. Compose with lambdas (next module).
sections.tip: The 'erase-remove idiom' is iconic but ugly: `v.erase(remove_if(...), v.end())`. C++20 simplified it to `std::erase_if(v, predicate)` — use that when available.

key_points: std::vector is your default container — contiguous memory, cache-friendly
std::map/set is O(log n) ordered; std::unordered_map/set is O(1) average
Iterators generalize pointers; end() is one-past-the-end (don't dereference)
map["key"] inserts default value if missing — use find() or contains()
STL algorithms in <algorithm> work on any iterator range
Iterator invalidation rules differ per container — read the docs
C++20 erase_if(container, predicate) replaces the erase-remove idiom

module_quiz.question: Which STL container should you reach for by default?
module_quiz.explanation: vector has contiguous storage, excellent cache behavior, and amortized O(1) push_back. It's faster than list even for many insertion patterns.

module_quiz.question: What does `myMap["missing_key"]` do if the key doesn't exist?
options: Default-constructs and inserts the value, then returns a reference
module_quiz.explanation: operator[] always inserts. Use `at()` (throws), `find()`, or `contains()` (C++20) to check without inserting.

module_quiz.question: What's the average complexity of unordered_map lookup?
module_quiz.explanation: Hash-based, so O(1) average. Worst case is O(n) with pathological hash collisions, but well-designed hash functions make this rare.

module_quiz.question: What does std::remove_if actually do?
options: Erases matching elements from the container
Reorders elements: moves keepers to the front, returns iterator to the new logical end — does not change container size
module_quiz.explanation: Algorithms can't change container size — they only see iterators. You must call erase() to actually shrink the container. Hence the erase-remove idiom.

module_quiz.question: Which container has random-access iterators?
module_quiz.explanation: vector and array (and deque, mostly) support O(1) jump-by-N. list is bidirectional; map/set are bidirectional; unordered are forward-only.

modules.title: Modern C++ (C++11/14/17/20): Lambdas, auto, Move Semantics

sections.heading: auto and Type Deduction
sections.content: `auto` lets the compiler infer a variable's type from its initializer. It's not 'dynamic typing' — the type is fixed at compile time, you just don't have to spell it out. Especially valuable for verbose iterator types and lambdas. Use `auto&` for references, `const auto&` for const references, `auto*` for pointers, `auto&&` for forwarding references. C++14 extended this to lambda parameters and return types.
sections.tip: Use auto for types that are obvious from context, especially iterators and lambdas. Don't use auto where the type matters for documentation (e.g., `auto count = getCount();` — is it int or size_t?).

sections.heading: Lambdas: Anonymous Functions
sections.content: Lambdas are inline anonymous functions, perfect for STL algorithms and callbacks. Syntax: `[capture](params) -> return_type { body }`. The capture list controls which surrounding variables the lambda can use. `[]` captures nothing, `[=]` captures all by value, `[&]` by reference, `[x]` x by value, `[&x]` x by reference. Use `mutable` if you need to modify by-value captures. C++14 added init-captures and generic lambdas; C++17 added constexpr lambdas; C++20 added template parameters.
sections.tip: Avoid `[=]` and `[&]` 'catch-all' captures in non-trivial lambdas. They hide what's actually captured and can cause dangling-reference bugs. Be explicit about each capture.

sections.heading: rvalue References and Move Semantics
sections.content: Pre-C++11, passing big objects to functions or returning them caused expensive copies. Move semantics fix this: if you don't need the source object anymore, just transfer its internals (pointers, etc.) instead of copying. An rvalue reference (`T&&`) binds to temporaries and moved-from objects. `std::move` is a cast: it tells the compiler 'treat this lvalue as an rvalue, you can steal from it'. Containers like std::vector and std::string have move constructors that swap pointers in O(1), avoiding O(n) copies.
sections.tip: Mark move constructors and move assignment `noexcept`. STL containers (vector, etc.) check this and fall back to copying if not noexcept — silently destroying your performance.

sections.heading: Perfect Forwarding and Universal References
sections.content: When writing template functions that pass arguments to other functions, you want to preserve whether each argument was an lvalue or rvalue. `T&&` in a template (where T is being deduced) is a 'forwarding reference' — it binds to anything. Combine with `std::forward<T>(arg)` to forward the original value category. This is how `std::make_unique` and `emplace_back` work: they construct the object in place by forwarding constructor arguments.
sections.tip: Use emplace_back over push_back when constructing in place. push_back(T(args...)) builds a temporary and moves it; emplace_back(args...) constructs directly inside the container.

sections.heading: constexpr, noexcept, and Other Key Specifiers
sections.content: `constexpr` declares a function or variable that can be evaluated at compile time — useful for performance-critical constants and template metaprogramming. C++17 added `if constexpr` for compile-time branching. `noexcept` declares a function won't throw — used by the standard library to enable optimizations. `[[nodiscard]]` warns if a return value is ignored — great for error codes and resource handles. C++20 added `consteval` (must be compile-time) and `constinit` (compile-time initialization).
sections.tip: Use `[[nodiscard]]` on functions whose return value is the whole point — error codes, parsed results, RAII handles. It catches a class of 'I forgot to check the return' bugs at compile time.

key_points: auto reduces verbosity for iterators and lambdas without losing type safety
Lambdas: [capture](args) { body }; explicit captures > [=] / [&] catch-alls
Move semantics avoid copies via rvalue references (T&&) and std::move
Mark move ctor/assign noexcept or std::vector won't move them
emplace_back > push_back when constructing in place
constexpr enables compile-time computation
[[nodiscard]] catches ignored return values at compile time

module_quiz.question: What does std::move actually do?
options: Physically moves data to a new memory location
It's a cast that allows the value to be treated as an rvalue, enabling move-instead-of-copy
module_quiz.explanation: std::move just performs a static_cast to T&&. Whether anything actually 'moves' depends on the receiving function having a move constructor.

module_quiz.question: Why mark move operations noexcept?
options: STL containers will use them only if noexcept; otherwise they fall back to (slower) copy
module_quiz.explanation: vector::resize, etc., need strong exception guarantees. Without noexcept moves, it copies instead — silently undermining performance.

module_quiz.question: What does emplace_back do that push_back doesn't?
options: Forwards constructor arguments to construct the element in place — no temporary
module_quiz.explanation: emplace_back(args) constructs T directly inside the container's memory. push_back(T(args)) creates a temporary first.

module_quiz.question: What's the difference between [=] and [&] capture?
options: [=] captures all referenced variables by value (copy); [&] captures by reference
[=] captures only ints; [&] captures only references
module_quiz.explanation: [=] makes copies (safer, but can be expensive); [&] takes references (fast but risks dangling refs if the lambda outlives the captured variables).

module_quiz.question: What does constexpr enable?
options: Compile-time evaluation, so the result can be used where a constant is required (like static_assert or array sizes)
module_quiz.explanation: constexpr functions can run at compile time given compile-time inputs. The compiler embeds the precomputed value, enabling things like array sizes.

modules.title: Templates & Generic Programming

sections.heading: Function Templates
sections.content: Templates let you write code parameterized by types. `template<typename T>` means 'for any type T, generate this function'. The compiler stamps out a separate version for each concrete type used — that's why templates must live in headers (the compiler needs to see the body when instantiating). Function templates enable algorithms like std::max, std::swap, and std::sort to work on any compatible type.
sections.tip: Templates are compile-time. A template error message is famously verbose because the compiler shows the entire instantiation chain. C++20 concepts (next section) clean this up dramatically.

sections.heading: Class Templates
sections.content: Class templates parameterize entire classes by type. The most familiar examples are STL containers: `std::vector<int>`, `std::map<string, int>`. Writing your own is straightforward — same `template<typename T>` prefix, then T appears wherever you need the type. C++17 added Class Template Argument Deduction (CTAD): you can write `std::pair p(1, "hi")` instead of `std::pair<int, const char*> p(1, "hi")`.
sections.tip: Define class template member functions inside the class body, or in the same header. Putting them in a .cpp file causes 'undefined reference' linker errors for any instantiation outside that .cpp.

sections.heading: Variadic Templates and Parameter Packs
sections.content: Variadic templates accept any number of type arguments. `template<typename... Ts>` declares a parameter pack. You expand it with `Ts...` (types) or `args...` (values). Combined with recursion or fold expressions (C++17 `(args + ...)`), they enable type-safe printf, generic factories, tuple, std::variant, and much more.
sections.tip: Fold expressions (C++17) made variadic templates approachable. Before that, you had to write recursive base + recursive case helpers — which still works but is verbose.

sections.heading: Concepts (C++20)
sections.content: Concepts are named compile-time predicates on types. They constrain templates: 'this T must be addable, comparable, hashable...'. The huge wins: error messages become clear ('T does not satisfy Addable' vs 100 lines of 'no operator+ found in type ...'), and they enable function overloading by concept. The standard library ships many useful concepts in `<concepts>`: integral, floating_point, copyable, movable, equality_comparable.
sections.tip: Even if you don't write your own concepts, just constraining templates with standard ones (std::integral, std::ranges::range) drastically improves error messages for callers.

key_points: Templates are compile-time — must be defined in headers, not .cpp files
Each instantiation produces a new copy of the code with concrete types
Class templates parameterize whole classes (e.g., std::vector<T>)
Variadic templates with fold expressions (C++17) handle any-arity functions
Concepts (C++20) constrain templates and make error messages readable
C++17 CTAD lets you skip explicit type args (std::pair p(1, "x"))

module_quiz.question: Why must template definitions usually live in header files?
options: The compiler needs the definition visible at every point of instantiation — separate .cpp would cause linker errors
module_quiz.explanation: When a translation unit instantiates a template with new types, the compiler needs to see the body. Otherwise, the symbol won't exist at link time.

module_quiz.question: What's a fold expression in C++17?
options: A concise way to apply a binary operator across a parameter pack: (args + ...)
module_quiz.explanation: Fold expressions replace recursive variadic template patterns with one-liners like `(args + ...)` or `(... + args)`.

module_quiz.question: What's the main practical benefit of C++20 concepts?
options: Constrain template parameters AND produce dramatically clearer compile errors
module_quiz.explanation: Constraints are checked at the call site, so errors say 'T doesn't satisfy Addable' instead of 100 lines of instantiation depth.

module_quiz.question: What does CTAD (C++17) let you do?
options: Construct templated classes without specifying type arguments — they're deduced from constructor arguments
module_quiz.explanation: Class Template Argument Deduction lets you write `std::pair p(1, 2.0)` instead of `std::pair<int, double> p(1, 2.0)`.

module_quiz.question: What is `template<typename... Args>`?
options: Variable-arity template — accepts any number of type parameters (parameter pack)
module_quiz.explanation: The `...` indicates a parameter pack. You expand it inside with Args... or args... — used in std::tuple, std::variant, make_shared, etc.

modules.title: Exceptions & Error Handling Strategies

sections.heading: throw, try, catch — The Mechanics
sections.content: C++ exceptions propagate up the call stack until caught by a matching `catch` block. Throw any object (typically derived from std::exception). The destructor of every object on the way out runs automatically — that's why RAII and exceptions work so well together. Catch by `const reference` to avoid slicing and unnecessary copies. Order catch blocks from most-derived to most-base type. Don't catch-and-rethrow without reason — it loses information.
sections.tip: Always catch exceptions by `const reference`. By value causes object slicing (catching a base by value strips the derived part). Pointers force you to manage lifetime — bad.

sections.heading: Exception Safety Guarantees
sections.content: Functions provide one of four exception-safety levels: (1) No-throw — never throws (mark with `noexcept`); (2) Strong — if it throws, state is unchanged (commit-or-rollback); (3) Basic — if it throws, no leaks/invariants intact, but state may have changed; (4) None — undefined behavior. Aim for at least Basic. Achieve Strong via the 'copy-and-swap' idiom or by performing all mutating work first and committing only after success.
sections.tip: Containers like std::vector give strong-exception-safety guarantees as long as the contained type's move/copy is noexcept. Yet another reason to mark moves noexcept.

sections.heading: Error Codes vs Exceptions vs std::expected
sections.content: Exceptions aren't free — they have a small runtime cost when thrown and add code-size overhead. Some domains (game engines, embedded, kernel) ban them outright. Alternatives: error codes (return value indicates success/failure), output parameters, std::optional (T or nothing), std::variant<T, ErrorType>, and C++23's std::expected<T, E>. Choose based on context: rare exceptional failures → exceptions; expected failures (parse errors, lookup misses) → optional/expected.
sections.tip: Throw exceptions for unexpected failures (out-of-memory, programmer errors). For expected failures (parsing user input, key not found), prefer optional or expected — they're explicit in the function signature.

sections.heading: noexcept and Stack Unwinding
sections.content: Marking a function `noexcept` is both documentation and an optimization. The compiler can omit unwinding tables and skip exception-handling overhead. Crucially, std::vector and other containers check `noexcept` on move operations to decide whether to move (fast) or copy (slow but safe) when reallocating. If a `noexcept` function throws anyway, std::terminate is called — your program dies. Destructors are implicitly noexcept; never throw from them.
sections.tip: Never throw from a destructor. If two destructors are running simultaneously (from stack unwinding), and one throws, std::terminate is called immediately.

key_points: Catch exceptions by const reference to avoid slicing and copies
Order catch blocks most-derived-first
Aim for at least basic exception safety; strong via copy-and-swap
Mark moves noexcept so containers can move (not copy) on reallocation
Never throw from destructors — std::terminate is called
Use optional/expected for expected failures, exceptions for unexpected
noexcept is both documentation and an optimization

module_quiz.question: Why catch exceptions by const reference?
options: Avoids slicing (preserves derived type) and avoids unnecessary copies
module_quiz.explanation: Catching by value would slice off derived parts. By pointer requires lifetime management. const reference is the idiomatic choice.

module_quiz.question: What happens if a noexcept function throws?
options: std::terminate is called and the program dies
module_quiz.explanation: noexcept is a hard promise. Violating it terminates the program — designed to make optimizations safe.

module_quiz.question: What's the strong exception-safety guarantee?
options: If the function throws, the program state is unchanged from before the call (commit-or-rollback)
module_quiz.explanation: Strong = either succeeds, or appears to never have run. Achieved via patterns like copy-and-swap.

module_quiz.question: When should you prefer std::optional or std::expected over exceptions?
options: For 'expected' failures like parsing or lookup misses, where the failure path is part of normal flow
module_quiz.explanation: Exceptions are for exceptional conditions. Routine 'this might fail' results are clearer in the signature with optional/expected.

module_quiz.question: Why is throwing from a destructor dangerous?
options: Destructors can't throw at all (compile error)
If one is already running due to stack unwinding from another exception, std::terminate is called
It's slower than throwing from a normal function
module_quiz.explanation: Two simultaneous active exceptions trigger std::terminate. Hence destructors are implicitly noexcept and must not throw.

modules.title: Multithreading & Concurrency

sections.heading: std::thread — Creating and Joining Threads
sections.content: C++11 added a portable threading API. `std::thread` constructs a thread with a callable. You must `join()` (wait for it to finish) or `detach()` (let it run in the background) before the thread object is destroyed — otherwise std::terminate is called. C++20 added `std::jthread` which auto-joins on destruction and supports cooperative cancellation via `std::stop_token`.
sections.tip: Forgetting to join() a thread terminates the program. Use std::jthread (C++20) or wrap std::thread in an RAII class — the manual join() pattern is bug-prone.

sections.heading: Mutexes and lock_guard
sections.content: When multiple threads access shared data and at least one writes, you have a data race — undefined behavior. Protect with a mutex. Always lock with RAII (`std::lock_guard` or `std::scoped_lock`) — never call `mutex.lock()` and `mutex.unlock()` manually. `std::scoped_lock` (C++17) takes multiple mutexes at once and acquires them deadlock-free using a clever ordering. `std::shared_mutex` allows multiple readers OR one writer.
sections.tip: Never call lock()/unlock() manually — an early return or exception leaks the lock. Always use lock_guard/unique_lock/scoped_lock. RAII applies to locks too.

sections.heading: Atomics: Lock-Free Synchronization
sections.content: `std::atomic<T>` provides lock-free read-modify-write operations on simple types (int, pointers, etc.). Operations like `++`, `compare_exchange`, `fetch_add` happen as a single, indivisible step — no mutex needed. Performance: atomic ops are faster than mutexes for simple counters but still slower than non-atomic. Memory ordering (relaxed, acquire/release, sequentially consistent) controls how operations on different atomics are ordered across threads — getting this right is genuinely hard. Default to seq_cst unless you've benchmarked and understood the relaxed semantics.
sections.tip: Lock-free isn't free — atomic operations cost cache-line ping-pong. For high contention, well-designed locks beat naively-atomic counters. Profile before optimizing.

sections.heading: Futures, Promises, and async
sections.content: `std::async` runs a function asynchronously and returns a `std::future<T>` for retrieving the result. `future.get()` blocks until the value is ready. `std::promise` is the producer side: a thread sets the value, another thread waits on the corresponding future. These are higher-level than raw threads and integrate well with one-shot result patterns. For complex pipelines, libraries like Boost.Asio, libdispatch, or oneTBB are more suitable.
sections.tip: std::async without an explicit launch policy may run synchronously (deferred). Always pass std::launch::async if you actually want a new thread.

sections.heading: Common Concurrency Bugs
sections.content: Concurrency bugs are notoriously hard to reproduce. Top sins: data races (unsynchronized access — UB even if the race seems harmless), deadlocks (circular lock-acquisition), livelocks (threads constantly retrying without progress), priority inversion, double-checked locking written incorrectly, and missing memory barriers. Tools that help: ThreadSanitizer (`-fsanitize=thread`), AddressSanitizer, and Valgrind's helgrind. Design strategies: prefer immutability, message-passing over shared state, std::atomic for simple counters, and minimize critical-section size.
sections.tip: Run your test suite under ThreadSanitizer in CI. It catches data races and lock-ordering issues that traditional tests will not — they only show up under specific scheduling.

key_points: Always join() (or detach()) std::thread before destruction — std::jthread auto-joins
Lock with RAII (lock_guard, scoped_lock) — never lock()/unlock() manually
std::scoped_lock (C++17) acquires multiple mutexes deadlock-free
std::shared_mutex allows multi-reader/single-writer
std::atomic<T> for lock-free simple operations; default to seq_cst memory order
std::async + future for one-shot async results
Compile and test with -fsanitize=thread to catch data races

module_quiz.question: What happens if you destroy a std::thread without joining or detaching?
options: The thread runs in the background
std::terminate is called and the program aborts
module_quiz.explanation: C++ enforces explicit lifecycle decisions for threads. Use std::jthread (C++20) for automatic joining.

module_quiz.question: Why never call mutex.lock() and mutex.unlock() manually?
options: An early return or exception will skip unlock(), leaking the lock — RAII (lock_guard) prevents this
module_quiz.explanation: Manual lock/unlock breaks under any non-linear control flow. RAII guarantees unlock at scope exit.

module_quiz.question: What does std::scoped_lock(a, b) provide that two separate lock_guards don't?
options: Deadlock-free acquisition of multiple mutexes via a deadlock-avoidance algorithm
module_quiz.explanation: scoped_lock locks all mutexes using std::lock under the hood, which guarantees no deadlock between threads acquiring the same set.

module_quiz.question: Is incrementing an int from two threads without synchronization safe?
options: Yes — increment is atomic on x86
No — it's a data race, which is undefined behavior regardless of platform
Only if the int is volatile
Yes if you use ++ instead of +=
module_quiz.explanation: The C++ memory model says concurrent unsynchronized read-modify-write is UB. Use std::atomic<int> or a mutex.

module_quiz.question: What's the typical use of std::shared_mutex?
options: Allowing multiple readers OR one writer — useful when reads massively outnumber writes
module_quiz.explanation: Reader-writer lock pattern. shared_lock for reads, unique_lock for writes. Adds overhead, so only worthwhile when read:write ratio is high.

modules.title: Performance, Undefined Behavior & Optimization

sections.heading: Undefined Behavior — The Most Dangerous Part of C++
sections.content: Undefined Behavior (UB) means the standard places no constraints on what your program does. It's not 'sometimes wrong' — it's a contract you've broken, and the compiler is free to do anything: crash, return garbage, delete files, exhibit different behavior on each run, or appear to work in debug and corrupt data in release. Common UB: signed integer overflow, dereferencing null/dangling pointers, out-of-bounds array access, data races, reading uninitialized variables, modifying string literals, violating object lifetime rules, infinite loops without side effects.
sections.tip: Compile your CI builds with -fsanitize=undefined,address and run all tests under them. UBSan and ASan catch most UB at runtime — the bugs that 'work in dev' otherwise.

sections.heading: Compiler Optimizations: Inlining, Vectorization, Devirtualization
sections.content: Modern C++ compilers (GCC, Clang, MSVC) are extraordinarily good. Optimization levels: `-O0` (none, debug), `-O2` (balanced, ship default), `-O3` (aggressive, sometimes worse due to code bloat), `-Os` (size), `-Ofast` (relaxes IEEE — for numerical code only). Key optimizations: inlining (small functions become part of caller), loop vectorization (SIMD instructions), constant folding, dead-code elimination, devirtualization (virtual call → direct call when type is provable), and link-time optimization (`-flto`, optimizes across translation units).
sections.tip: Don't try to outsmart the optimizer with micro-tricks. Write clean idiomatic C++; compilers turn it into great assembly. Measure (perf, vtune, callgrind) before optimizing.

sections.heading: Cache Locality and Data-Oriented Design
sections.content: Modern CPUs are fast; main memory is slow. A cache miss costs 100-300 cycles. The biggest performance lever in modern code isn't algorithmic — it's keeping the working set hot in cache. Prefer contiguous structures (vector, array) over linked structures (list, map). For object-heavy code, consider 'Structure of Arrays' (parallel vectors of fields) over 'Array of Structures' — game engines and graphics code use this heavily. False sharing (two threads writing to neighboring cache lines) silently destroys multi-threaded performance.
sections.tip: Profile first. The naive AoS version is usually fast enough. Only restructure to SoA after measuring a real cache-miss bottleneck — readability matters too.

sections.heading: Profiling and Benchmarking
sections.content: 'Premature optimization is the root of all evil' — but profiling is mandatory before optimizing. Sampling profilers (perf, VTune, Instruments) tell you where time is spent. Benchmark frameworks like Google Benchmark or Catch2's `BENCHMARK` measure tiny code paths reliably (they handle warmup, statistical noise, prevent dead-code elimination). Always benchmark optimized release builds with realistic data — micro-benchmarks of debug builds are misleading.
sections.tip: Always include benchmark::DoNotOptimize and benchmark::ClobberMemory in microbenchmarks. Without them, the compiler will often delete your benchmark's body since the result is unused.

key_points: Undefined Behavior is a contract violation — the compiler may do ANYTHING
Signed overflow, OOB access, use-after-free, data races are all UB
Run UBSan and ASan in CI: -fsanitize=undefined,address
-O2 is the standard ship optimization level; -O3 sometimes worsens performance
Cache misses (100+ cycles) dominate modern performance
Prefer contiguous containers (vector) for cache locality
SoA can outperform AoS for column-heavy operations and SIMD
Profile before optimizing; benchmark with -O2+ and realistic data

module_quiz.question: What does Undefined Behavior mean in C++?
options: The behavior depends on the compiler
The behavior is unpredictable but consistent
The standard places no constraints; the compiler may do anything
It's the same as a runtime error
module_quiz.explanation: UB licenses the compiler to assume it never happens — leading to optimizations that produce nonsense if you trigger UB.

module_quiz.question: Which is well-defined in C++ rather than UB?
module_quiz.explanation: Unsigned arithmetic is defined to wrap modulo 2^N. Signed overflow is UB — and compilers exploit this for optimization.

module_quiz.question: Why does std::vector typically beat std::list for sum operations even with frequent insertions?
options: Cache locality — contiguous memory means hardware prefetcher streams data; list jumps around memory
module_quiz.explanation: Modern CPUs love sequential memory access. List nodes scattered across heap addresses cause constant cache misses.

module_quiz.question: Which sanitizer should you enable to catch use-after-free and OOB access?
module_quiz.explanation: ASan tracks heap and stack memory and catches OOB, UAF, double-free. Combine with UBSan in CI for broad coverage.

module_quiz.question: What is false sharing?
options: When threads see different values for a shared variable
When two threads write to different variables that happen to live on the same cache line, causing cache invalidation overhead
Sharing data over the network falsely
module_quiz.explanation: Independent variables on the same cache line force the cache coherence protocol to bounce the line between cores. Align hot per-thread data to its own cache line.

modules.title: Build Systems, Tools & Modern Workflows

sections.heading: CMake — The De Facto Build System
sections.content: C++ has no built-in build tool. Decades of options (Make, Autotools, SCons, Bazel, Meson) have largely converged on CMake. CMake generates platform-native build files (Makefiles on Linux, Ninja, MSVC projects, Xcode). Modern (3.15+) CMake is target-based: each library/executable is a target with its own properties (include dirs, compile flags, dependencies). Avoid the ancient style of global variables — favor `target_*` functions everywhere.
sections.code:
# CMakeLists.txt — modern CMake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)
project(MyApp VERSION 1.0 LANGUAGES CXX)

set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
set(CMAKE_CXX_EXTENSIONS OFF)

# Library
add_library(mylib STATIC
    src/parser.cpp
    src/serializer.cpp
)
target_include_directories(mylib PUBLIC include)
target_compile_features(mylib PUBLIC cxx_std_20)
target_compile_options(mylib PRIVATE -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic)

# Executable
add_executable(myapp src/main.cpp)
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE mylib)

# Tests with CTest
enable_testing()
add_executable(tests tests/test_main.cpp)
target_link_libraries(tests PRIVATE mylib)
add_test(NAME unit_tests COMMAND tests)

# Build commands:
# $ cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -G Ninja
# $ cmake --build build -j
# $ ctest --test-dir build --output-on-failure
sections.tip: Treat CMakeLists.txt like real code. Use modern target-based commands (target_link_libraries, target_include_directories) and PRIVATE/PUBLIC/INTERFACE visibility correctly. Avoid include_directories() and other global commands.

sections.heading: Package Management: vcpkg and Conan
sections.content: C++ historically lacked a package manager, leading to manual dependency hell. Today there are good options. `vcpkg` (Microsoft) integrates with CMake via toolchain file — `vcpkg install fmt` then use `find_package(fmt)`. `Conan` is more flexible, has its own profiles and lockfiles. Both have thousands of common libraries (boost, fmt, spdlog, gtest, openssl, etc.). Use one of them — manually downloading and building each dependency is no longer worth it.
sections.code:
# vcpkg workflow
# $ git clone https://github.com/microsoft/vcpkg
# $ ./vcpkg/bootstrap-vcpkg.sh
# $ ./vcpkg/vcpkg install fmt spdlog gtest

# Then in CMake:
# $ cmake -S . -B build \
#     -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=./vcpkg/scripts/buildsystems/vcpkg.cmake

# In CMakeLists.txt:
# find_package(fmt CONFIG REQUIRED)
# find_package(spdlog CONFIG REQUIRED)
# target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE fmt::fmt spdlog::spdlog)

# Conan workflow (conanfile.txt)
# [requires]
# fmt/10.1.1
# spdlog/1.12.0
# gtest/1.14.0
#
# [generators]
# CMakeDeps
# CMakeToolchain
#
# $ conan install . --output-folder=build --build=missing
# $ cmake --preset conan-release
# $ cmake --build build/Release
sections.tip: Pin dependency versions. vcpkg has manifest mode (vcpkg.json) and Conan has lockfiles — use them so your CI builds the exact same versions every time.

sections.heading: Static Analysis and Linters: clang-tidy, cppcheck
sections.content: Static analyzers catch bug classes the compiler doesn't. `clang-tidy` is the heavyweight — hundreds of checks, modernization suggestions, and code-style enforcement. `cppcheck` is faster, simpler, finds different issues. Run both in CI. Use `clang-format` for consistent style across a team — pick a base style (LLVM, Google, Mozilla) and customize via `.clang-format`. `include-what-you-use` (IWYU) trims unused includes.
sections.code:
# Sample .clang-tidy config
Checks: >
  *,
  -fuchsia-*,
  -google-readability-*,
  -llvmlibc-*,
  -altera-*
WarningsAsErrors: 'modernize-*,bugprone-*'
HeaderFilterRegex: '.*'

# Run on a file
# $ clang-tidy src/main.cpp -- -std=c++20

# Run on the whole project (with compile_commands.json)
# $ cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON
# $ run-clang-tidy -p build src/

# .clang-format example
# BasedOnStyle: LLVM
# IndentWidth: 4
# ColumnLimit: 100
# PointerAlignment: Left

# Run clang-format
# $ clang-format -i src/*.cpp src/*.h

# cppcheck
# $ cppcheck --enable=all --inconclusive --suppress=missingInclude src/
sections.tip: Generate compile_commands.json from CMake (-DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON). Almost every C++ tool consumes it: clang-tidy, IDE indexers, sanitizer runners, and IWYU.

sections.heading: Testing: GoogleTest, Catch2, doctest
sections.content: C++ testing frameworks have matured. `GoogleTest` (gtest) is the most widely used — TEST/EXPECT/ASSERT macros, mocks via gmock, well-documented. `Catch2` and `doctest` are header-only, simpler syntax (TEST_CASE/SECTION), great for small projects. Pair with `ctest` to run from CMake. Test categories you should write: unit (single function), integration (multiple components), and end-to-end (the system as a user sees it). Aim for fast tests so devs run them locally.
sections.tip: Run tests under sanitizers in CI (separate job for ASan, UBSan, TSan). Real-world bugs live in undefined behavior that only sanitizers catch — your tests pass without them but production crashes.

sections.heading: CI/CD and Cross-Platform Builds
sections.content: Modern C++ projects build and test on Linux, macOS, and Windows in CI on every PR. GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and CircleCI all support matrix builds. Run on multiple compilers (GCC, Clang, MSVC), multiple standards (C++17, C++20), debug + release, with various sanitizer combinations. Cache CMake build directories and dependency installs (vcpkg/conan) to keep CI fast. Build artifacts: package release builds with CPack (CMake's tool) into .deb, .rpm, .zip, or installers.
sections.code:
# .github/workflows/build.yml
name: build
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-22.04, macos-latest, windows-latest]
        compiler: [gcc, clang]
        build_type: [Debug, Release]
        exclude:
          - os: windows-latest
            compiler: gcc
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install vcpkg
        run: |
          git clone https://github.com/microsoft/vcpkg
          ./vcpkg/bootstrap-vcpkg.sh
      - name: Configure
        run: cmake -S . -B build \
               -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=vcpkg/scripts/buildsystems/vcpkg.cmake \
               -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${{ matrix.build_type }}
      - name: Build
        run: cmake --build build -j
      - name: Test
        run: ctest --test-dir build --output-on-failure

  sanitize:
    runs-on: ubuntu-22.04
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Configure with sanitizers
        run: cmake -S . -B build \
               -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug \
               -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer"
      - run: cmake --build build -j && ctest --test-dir build
sections.tip: Add a separate sanitizer job to CI (ASan + UBSan, then a TSan job). Don't add them to all jobs — they slow tests significantly and can produce false alarms in some libraries.

key_points: Modern CMake is target-based: target_link_libraries, target_include_directories
Use a package manager (vcpkg or Conan) instead of vendoring deps manually
Run clang-tidy and cppcheck in CI for static analysis
Use clang-format for consistent style across the team
GoogleTest, Catch2, and doctest are the popular testing frameworks
Run tests under ASan/UBSan/TSan in CI, in addition to plain builds
Build matrix across compilers + OSes catches cross-platform issues early
Generate compile_commands.json for IDE/tooling integration

module_quiz.question: Which is preferred in modern CMake?
module_quiz.explanation: Target-based commands scope properties to specific targets and propagate them via PUBLIC/INTERFACE — the modern, correct approach.

module_quiz.question: What does compile_commands.json provide?
options: Per-file compile invocations consumed by clang-tidy, IDE indexers, and other tools
module_quiz.explanation: It records exactly how each source file is compiled. Generated by CMake with -DCMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS=ON.

module_quiz.question: Which is a C++ package manager?
module_quiz.explanation: vcpkg (Microsoft) and Conan are the leading C++ package managers. npm is for JS, pip for Python, cargo for Rust.

module_quiz.question: Why run sanitizers in CI?
options: They detect undefined behavior, memory bugs, and data races that regular tests miss
module_quiz.explanation: Many bugs only manifest as UB at runtime under specific input. ASan, UBSan, and TSan catch them mechanically.

module_quiz.question: What does clang-format do?
options: Re-formats source according to a style configuration, ensuring consistent code style across a team
module_quiz.explanation: clang-format reformats source per a .clang-format config. Run automatically (pre-commit hook or CI) to eliminate style debates.

quiz.question: Who created C++?
quiz.explanation: Bjarne Stroustrup designed C++ at Bell Labs, starting in 1979 as 'C with Classes'.

quiz.question: Which header provides std::cout and std::cin?
quiz.explanation: <iostream> provides the standard streams. <stdio.h> is C's printf/scanf.

quiz.question: What does 'std::' stand for in 'std::cout'?
quiz.explanation: All standard-library names live in the std namespace.

quiz.question: What does the `auto` keyword do?
options: Tells the compiler to deduce a variable's type from its initializer
quiz.explanation: auto is type deduction, fixed at compile time — not dynamic typing.

quiz.question: Which is the modern C++ null pointer literal?
quiz.explanation: C++11 added nullptr, type-safe and avoids ambiguity with int 0.

quiz.question: What's the size of `bool` in C++?
quiz.explanation: C++ requires sizeof(bool) >= 1. Most implementations use 1 byte.

quiz.question: Which container provides O(1) average-case lookup?
quiz.explanation: Hash-based unordered_map gives O(1) average. map is O(log n).

quiz.question: Which loop iterates over all elements of a container in modern C++?
quiz.explanation: Range-based for (C++11) is concise and works with any container exposing begin/end.

quiz.question: What does sizeof return?
options: Number of elements in an array
quiz.explanation: sizeof yields the size in bytes (specifically, in `char` units, where char is 1 by definition).

quiz.question: Which is the C++ standard input stream?
quiz.explanation: std::cin reads from stdin. std::cout writes to stdout, std::cerr to stderr.

quiz.question: Which smart pointer should you reach for first?
quiz.explanation: unique_ptr expresses sole ownership with zero overhead. auto_ptr was removed in C++17.

quiz.question: What is the Rule of Five?
options: If you write any of {destructor, copy ctor, copy assign, move ctor, move assign} you usually need all five
Have at most 5 members per class
quiz.explanation: Special member functions interact — defining one usually means you need to define (or = default / = delete) the others.

quiz.question: Why catch exceptions by const reference?
options: Avoids object slicing and unnecessary copies
quiz.explanation: By value slices off derived parts. const& preserves the dynamic type without copying.

quiz.question: What's wrong with returning a reference to a local variable?
options: Dangling reference — the local is destroyed at function exit, calling code accesses freed memory (UB)
quiz.explanation: Local objects die at scope exit. Returning a reference or pointer to one is undefined behavior.

quiz.question: What does `delete[] p;` differ from `delete p;`?
options: delete[] is for arrays allocated with new[], delete is for single objects allocated with new — mismatching is UB
quiz.explanation: Mixing is UB. Better: use std::vector and std::unique_ptr instead of raw new/delete.

quiz.question: When does std::vector invalidate iterators?
options: On reallocation (e.g., push_back beyond capacity), all iterators invalidate; on insert/erase, iterators at and after the position invalidate
quiz.explanation: Reallocation moves all elements to new memory. Reserve capacity if you want stable iterators.

quiz.question: What's std::move?
options: A cast that produces an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics
quiz.explanation: std::move is just static_cast<T&&>. It enables move-instead-of-copy when the receiving function has a move constructor.

quiz.question: Why mark move operations noexcept?
options: STL containers (e.g., vector::resize) only move noexcept types, falling back to copy otherwise
quiz.explanation: vector needs strong exception safety on resize — without noexcept moves, it copies, killing performance.

quiz.question: What does the `override` keyword do?
options: Forces a function to be virtual
Tells the compiler to verify this method actually overrides a base virtual method — catches signature-mismatch bugs
quiz.explanation: Without override, a typo silently creates a new function. With it, the compiler errors if no virtual base method matches.

quiz.question: What does it mean for a member function to be `const`?
options: The function promises not to modify *this — callable on const objects
quiz.explanation: const at the end of a member declaration locks down what the function can mutate, and is required to call it on const instances.

quiz.question: Which container is sorted by key automatically?
quiz.explanation: std::map (and std::set) maintain sorted order via a balanced BST. unordered_* uses a hash table — no order.

quiz.question: What's a pure virtual function?
options: A virtual function with no body
A virtual function declared = 0, making the class abstract — derived classes must implement it
A function that runs in pure mode
quiz.explanation: Adding `= 0` makes a function pure virtual; the class is then abstract (cannot be instantiated directly).

quiz.question: Which header provides std::thread?
quiz.explanation: std::thread, std::this_thread::sleep_for, and std::jthread all live in <thread>.

quiz.question: What is RAII the acronym for?
quiz.explanation: Resources (memory, locks, files) are acquired in constructors and released in destructors, tying lifetime to scope.

quiz.question: What does `explicit` on a constructor do?
options: Prevents implicit conversions — disables single-arg constructors being used in conversions
quiz.explanation: Without explicit, `Foo f = 5;` may silently call `Foo(int)`. With it, only `Foo f(5);` or `Foo f{5};` compile.

quiz.question: What does `using namespace std;` do at file scope?
options: Makes all names from std accessible without the std:: prefix — discouraged in headers due to name collisions
quiz.explanation: Convenient in small .cpp files, hazardous in headers — pollutes everyone's namespace and risks ADL surprises.

quiz.question: What does `constexpr` mean for a function?
options: Function may be evaluated at compile time when given compile-time arguments
quiz.explanation: constexpr functions usable both at compile time (where the result becomes a constant expression) and at runtime.

quiz.question: Why use `emplace_back` over `push_back` when constructing in place?
options: emplace_back forwards args to T's constructor in-place; push_back(T(args)) creates a temporary first
quiz.explanation: Skips a copy/move of the temporary. Especially useful for non-trivially-copyable types.

quiz.question: What is signed integer overflow in C++?
quiz.explanation: Unsigned overflow wraps. Signed overflow is UB — the optimizer assumes it can't happen.

quiz.question: What's the purpose of a virtual destructor?
options: Ensures `delete basePtr` calls the correct derived destructor when polymorphic deletion happens
quiz.explanation: Without it, deleting via base pointer skips derived destructors — silent resource leak.

quiz.question: Which comparison correctly compares two C-style strings?
quiz.explanation: == compares pointers, not contents. strcmp does character-by-character comparison; std::string handles == correctly.

quiz.question: What does the 'spaceship' operator <=> (C++20) provide?
options: A way to launch async tasks
quiz.explanation: Single operator that subsumes all six comparisons — `auto operator<=>(const T&) const = default;` covers everything.

quiz.question: What's the main benefit of std::scoped_lock over multiple std::lock_guards?
options: Acquires multiple mutexes deadlock-free using a try-lock-with-backoff algorithm
quiz.explanation: scoped_lock(a, b) won't deadlock with another scoped_lock(b, a) because it uses std::lock under the hood.

quiz.question: What is std::optional<T>?
options: A wrapper holding either a T value or no value (std::nullopt) — type-safe alternative to nullable pointers
quiz.explanation: Pre-C++17, you used special sentinel values or pointers. optional makes 'value or nothing' explicit in the type.

quiz.question: What is a forwarding (universal) reference?
options: T&& where T is a deduced template parameter — binds to anything; pair with std::forward to preserve value category
A reference that can be reseated
quiz.explanation: In `template<typename T> void f(T&& x)`, T&& binds to lvalues OR rvalues. Use std::forward<T>(x) to pass through correctly.

quiz.question: Why are templates typically defined in headers, not .cpp files?
options: The compiler needs the body at every instantiation — placing in .cpp causes 'undefined reference' linker errors
quiz.explanation: Each translation unit instantiating a template needs its body. Without it, no code is generated for that instantiation.

quiz.question: What does `[[nodiscard]]` do?
options: Compiler warns if the function's return value is discarded by the caller
quiz.explanation: Useful for error codes, parsed results, RAII handles — anything where ignoring the return is almost certainly a bug.

quiz.question: What is false sharing in multithreading?
options: Two threads writing to different variables that happen to be on the same cache line, causing cache-coherence overhead
quiz.explanation: Even though the variables are independent, the cache line ping-pongs between cores — silently destroys multi-threaded scaling.

quiz.question: Which is the C++ memory order considered sequentially consistent?
quiz.explanation: seq_cst is the strongest, easiest-to-reason-about ordering, the default. Use weaker orders only after benchmarking.

quiz.question: Which sanitizer would you use to catch concurrent data races?
quiz.explanation: TSan instruments memory accesses to detect unsynchronized concurrent reads/writes. Run your test suite under it in CI.
